text: The video shows the left side of the patient, revealing a large indirect inguinal hernia extending up to the deep internal ring.
The surgeon manually reduces a portion of the sigmoid colon that is involved in the incarcerated left inguinal scrotal hernia before anesthesia is administered.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions around the hernia site, avoiding manipulation of the flap until it is opened, and gently pushes surrounding tissues down without attempting to reduce the hernia cone.
The surgeon observes the hernia sac sitting at the deep internal ring and notes that it falls away when gentle traction is applied to the sac.
The surgeon navigates across the midline, maintaining dissection within the preperitoneal plane, which is visible as a distinct tissue layer beneath the peritoneum.
The camera reveals a large right direct inguinal hernia alongside a small right indirect inguinal hernia, indicating a pantaloon hernia on the right side.
The surgeon navigates into the supravesicular space, where the medial umbilical ligaments are visibly encased in a thick layer of yellow adipose tissue.
The right direct inguinal hernia is visible, extending into the supra pubic space.
The surgeon pulls down on the tissue, revealing the opening of tissue planes encased in fat, as they progress towards the preperitoneal plane.

The surgeon navigates across the top of the deeper internal ring, approaching the iliopubic tract. The dissection progresses through the supravesicular space, revealing the right direct inguinal hernia and the antisac coming into view.
The surgeon uses a cautery device to push away the anti-sac, applying several pulses to separate it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes a suprapubic hernia extending into the femoral space, noting that the surrounding extra fat prevents the tissue from naturally retracting.

The surgeon works laterally on a large indirect hernia, using electrocautery to dissect through the fat encasing the hernia sac. The hernia sac is visible to the left, with the surgeon carefully avoiding the spermatic cord vessels.

The surgeon opens the hernia sac to regain orientation, revealing the vas deferens on the right side. The surgical field shows the sac being manipulated as the surgeon navigates around the anatomical structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects laterally to take down the flap surrounding a sizable indirect hernia, inspecting the cord lipoma. The procedure involves managing bleeding due to the vascular nature of the cord lipoma in the fatty pelvic region.
The surgeon differentiates the cord lipoma from surrounding fat planes, visually identifying the distinct tissue structure amidst the other fat layers.

The surgeon reorients to the spermatic cord, which is obscured by surrounding fat. Using sweeping motions, the surgeon clears the fatty tissue to reveal the cord, noting the presence of a very fatty cord rather than a cord lipoma.

The surgeon pulls up the spermatic cord on the right side, carefully peeling away the surrounding tissue that has been committed for removal, revealing the cord as it separates from the adjacent structures.
The surgeon carefully peels away the tissue surrounding the cord lipoma, avoiding excessive dissection near the deep internal ring to prevent nerve damage.
The surgeon extracts a visible mass, likely a cord lipoma, from the surgical field and sets it aside for later processing.

The surgeon handles a large indirect hernia, having completed most of the medial dissection. They sweep the hernia down while carefully manipulating the superior portion of the flap to avoid transmitting thermal energy to the underlying colon.
The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down the iliopubic tract, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon works laterally, carefully avoiding the nerves while pushing the retroperitoneal fat laterally, maintaining a thin dissection plane.
The surgeon observes the colon beginning to drape down, indicating the progression of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon applies gentle cautery to the fat side of the hernia contents, avoiding the cord lipoma to protect the underlying colon.

The surgeon observes the colon positioned safely away from the surgical field and proceeds to carefully identify the hernia sac while leaving the cord lipoma intact. The surgeon examines the orientation of the structures to ensure safe manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the cord structures and maintains a high position to allow them to sweep down, observing a window in the tissue. The fatty appearance of the spermatic cord on the right side is noted, and the surgeon proceeds with caution, assuming the presence of a fatty spermatic cord.
The surgeon identifies a large vessel adjacent to the hernia sac, noting the vascular nature of the surrounding lipomas. The focus returns to examining the hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens on the right side, with fatty cord structures visible below. A large lipoma is observed sitting atop these structures, and the surgeon begins to peel it back.
The surgeon identifies the attenuated cord structures, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the hernia sac laterally and identifies the chord structures positioned more medially within the surgical field.
The surgeon pulls back tissue to reorient the surgical field, revealing the vas deferens coming into view.
The surgeon carefully sweeps the fat back to reveal nerves coming into view, while attempting to elevate the cord structures and pull the flap down.
The surgeon bullrings the mesh together in the center to prevent rubbing, while closing the center to reorient the medial umbilical ligament and adjacent fatty structures.

The surgeon fixates the cord lipoma and hernia sac onto the closure of the flap to prevent them from falling back into the pocket. The large inguinal scrotal hernia is visible, with extensive vascular tissue requiring careful management.

The surgeon sutures the cord lipoma or hernia sac onto the flap, ensuring fixation to prevent it from falling back into the pocket. The mesh is placed behind the pocket, and the inferior flap is pulled up to lay the mesh down, with additional fixation to secure the structures.
